26.1.label: Question 26.1
26.1.scenario: A leading provider of individual private medical insurance is based in a developed country with a comprehensive free State healthcare system. Dental treatment is offered free to the poorest in the country, children and the elderly. All other citizens are expected to contribute to their own treatment, although it is subsidised by the State. There is also a growing private market for dental treatment as the State system is put under increasing pressure through increased demand and lack of dentists. The company's marketing department believes that there is a significant opportunity to offer insurance for dental treatment in this market. Following initial discussions, the marketing director has suggested that the company should market a stand-alone dental insurance policy, with little or no formal medical underwriting, sold directly to the public, supported by radio advertising. The Board of Directors have asked for a discussion document addressing the issues the insurer needs to consider before launching such a product.
26.1.question: Describe the points that would be made in the discussion document, under the following headings: (i) benefits provided (ii) arrangements with dentists (iii) underwriting.

(i) Benefits provided: The policy should provide the following:
primary care, ie treatment provided by the patient's dentist
preventative care, such as routine check-ups, scale and polishes
secondary dental care, eg hospital-based operations for the extraction of wisdom teeth
tertiary care, eg manufacture of dentures.
Emergency treatment and accidental damage to teeth should also be covered.
In this arrangement, there is no 'gatekeeper role' (such as the GP for PMI claims) and so the insured or dentist may be tempted for there to be more treatment than is clinically necessary.
Those customers that buy policies will be more conscious of the need for dental care, and so might be more prone to this temptation.

Claims could be limited by insisting on co-insurance, eg the insured pays 50% of the cost of each treatment, and/or imposing a limit on payments in any policy year, eg a maximum annual payment of £250.
Annual excesses may also help to control claims and hence reduce premiums.

Policies with reduced premiums that exclude preventative care could be offered. However, this may not be in the insurer's long-term interest because timely preventative care may reduce claims for treatment.
Effectively discouraging preventative care may not be an image that the insurer wishes to cultivate.

In order to control claims for secondary and tertiary care, which could be substantial, the insurer could seek information and recommendations from the dentist before authorising a course of treatment.
Policies with reduced premiums that exclude claims for certain treatments, such as the provision of dentures, could be offered.
The insurer could consider offering 'family' policies that extend cover to a spouse and children. This would increase premium sizes and help to spread initial costs.
Child cover in particular would need to be carefully costed and controlled because dental claims for primary and preventative care may be substantial, eg problems with dental development.
Child cover may not be very attractive because the State offers free treatment for children.
(ii) Arrangements with dentists: There are two principal methods of providing dental cover:
the capitation basis, where the dentist is paid an agreed fixed sum per mouth insured
an insurance basis, where the insurer pays the cost of treatment in return for regular premiums.
The insurance basis would be most appropriate in this case, because the insurer is not selling through dentists and requires underwriting to be simple.
The insured could restrict cover to treatment by a list of authorised dentists with whom the insurer has contractual arrangements for providing care.
A carefully worded contract (service level agreement) should be drawn up between the insurer and each authorised dentist.
This should allow the insurer to negotiate fixed fees for treatments at competitive prices.
The insurer could insist on 'quality measures' as part of the contract, eg surgery hours outside normal working hours.
Dentists may be attracted to authorisation despite the conditions imposed by the insurer because they will obtain patients without the need for any form of marketing on their part.

The insurer could use the authorised list as a marketing tool, or a service for existing customers, by offering to register a policyholder with an approved dentist in a location of the insured's choice.
(iii) Underwriting: There is a significant opportunity for selection against the insurer.

Those who have not received such care recently and suspect they need considerable care (even though they are not in pain) could take out insurance with the intention of obtaining the care they need at the insurer's expense.
Obtaining the appropriate information for full medical underwriting will be difficult in practice.
Dentists keep their own records, and so the insurer would need to contact all of the proposer's (previous) dentists to obtain a full history.
A proposal form might ask about the most recent visits to a dentist and the treatment received, but the insured will not have the knowledge to provide specialist or precise details.
The costs of extensive initial underwriting would be a significant proportion of the premium.
If a dental insurance product is to have wide appeal, premiums, and consequently initial expenses, must be kept low.

Accept with no underwriting but only accept claims once the patient has had all necessary treatment certified by the patient's dentist. The difficulty here will be in monitoring the standards for acceptance.
Impose a moratorium on pre-existing conditions. However, this might be difficult to work for dental insurance because of the difficulty of specifying pre-existing conditions.
Impose a waiting period, say six months, before claims can be made. This may not be popular with policyholders, but would be simple and cheap.
In all cases, claims for accident or emergency treatment would be honoured from the start of the policy, as such claims would be deemed to be out of the insured's control.
The insurer could also consider a form of managed care, whereby the insured was required to have regular dental checks as a condition of insurance.
This would help to control longer-term claims costs and encourage responsible dental care by the insured.
This might also be popular with dentists on an authorised list who would receive a regular income stream.

26.2.label: Question 26.2
26.2.scenario: A firm of consulting actuaries are advising a small health and care insurance company in a developed country. The insurance company currently transacts a wide range of individual health business, sold through financial advisers. The annual volume of new business has been roughly unchanged for several years while the insurer's market share has declined. The insurer has decided to extend its product range. It is also considering whether to offer its new products through different distribution channels. It has identified three new types of healthcare product that it might develop: (a) immediate needs long-term care insurance providing regular cash payments, increasing in line with retail price inflation index (b) conventional group income protection insurance providing a pay-related benefit whilst unable to work as a result of accident or sickness (c) unit-linked major medical expenses insurance, offering a range of linked funds, and providing cash payments to policyholders when requiring a major surgical procedure. The insurer has asked for advice on a range of aspects regarding each product.
26.2.question: Discuss the points the consultants would make in their report for each product under the following headings: (i) a suitable underwriting policy (ii) appropriate reinsurance arrangements (iii) the main distribution channel(s) through which the product might be sold (iv) the main costs and risks associated with developing and launching the product.

Immediate needs long-term care insurance: Bear in mind that heavy mortality experience will benefit the insurer, although this will depend on the significance of any death benefit.
The insurer would normally charge a premium based on the individual health status of the proposer.
In order to assess this, they will need to collect detailed medical evidence, but this will be costly to do for each applicant.
An indicative quotation could be obtained, based on the answers to a small number of questions on the proposal form.
Activity-based, or functional ability, tests might be carried out to assess the applicant's level of disability.
If the proposal goes ahead, further medical evidence could then be obtained, from the applicant and their doctor.
This would be used to give a final premium for the individual concerned.
It is important that evidence is gathered and a decision is made quickly, because the applicants will be in need of care at the time of the proposal.
The use of medical examinations should be kept to a minimum as this is probably an unnecessary cost and would lead to further inconvenience for the proposer at a difficult time.
Group income protection (IP) insurance: Underwriting will need to be simple and in line with the market for group IP, in order that the cover is attractive to the employer and administration is kept to a minimum.
Free cover limits (or 'non-selection' limits) will be set. All employees requiring cover below the limit will be accepted without being individually underwritten.
The free cover limit from each group will depend on the size of the group (eg based on number of members or total salaries) and the proportion of employees taking up the cover.
The insurer may take other factors into account in setting the free cover limits, such as the industry or location of the group.
Typically the insurer will require that all insured members are 'actively at work' on the day that (or for a specified period before) the cover commences.
It may also insist that a waiting period applies for each new member, before a claim can be accepted.
It may insist that the benefits provided follow a prescribed formula (eg based on a percentage of salary).
Employees requesting cover above the free cover limits will be individually underwritten. They will need to provide medical information, undergo tests, etc.
The underwriter will also consider factors such as time spent in a dangerous environment or in overseas territories.
Special terms may be applied such as increased premiums on cover in excess of the free cover.
Unit-linked major medical expense (MME): The following forms of underwriting could be considered:
front-end underwriting, where the proposer is fully medically underwritten at outset and either a specific exclusion(s), or an increased morbidity charge, is applied

back-end underwriting, where no, or very limited, underwriting is carried out at proposal, but any pre-existing conditions are excluded from cover. This will be assessed once a pre-existing condition claim is made.
Back-end underwriting is probably more appropriate for this product - it will simplify the sales process.
This will make it easier to sell the product and also reduce initial expenses.
However claims assessment will be more involved and expensive.
The policy may exclude claims from, or related to, conditions that existed when the policy was taken out.
A moratorium (ie subsequently allowing claims if a condition has not arisen in the next, say, two years) could be considered, but this may over-complicate the product.
At the claims stage, medical evidence will be collected and assessed to ensure the validity of the claim (eg that the surgical procedure is medically necessary).
Medical history, including a GP report, will be required for the latter.

However, given the complexity of the product - due to its unit-linked nature - and that its main appeal may therefore be to financially-sophisticated customers, full initial underwriting may be acceptable to customers and sellers.

Immediate needs LTCI: Reinsurance will help to: protect against claim volatility, which will be uncertain, since this is a new product and volumes of business will be small; provide technical assistance, eg data for pricing and help with underwriting; reduce risk and hence reduce capital requirements.
The insurer is likely to arrange quota-share reinsurance, probably on original terms, in order to build up experience the first few years without taking too large a risk.
Group IP insurance: This insurer is likely to be writing significant volumes of IP business already. The requirement for reinsurance is likely to be small, although reinsurers may provide useful advice specific to group business, eg product design, underwriting and data for pricing.

Current reinsurance arrangements would have some bearing here. Original terms quota share is probably best as it is simple and level of cover will not vary significantly, eg usually between 60% and 75% of salary.
The proportion of business reinsured might reduce over time as experience develops.
The insurer may consider catastrophe reinsurance if it was concerned about a number of claims from the same employer.
Unit-linked MME insurance: The insurer should consider reinsurance as it will be concerned with uncertainty of claims experience and is likely to require technical assistance.
The investment risk is mainly borne by the customer, and so risk premium reinsurance would be appropriate for unit linked policies, perhaps with a high retention level.

(iii).question: The main distribution channel(s) through which the product might be sold

Immediate needs LTCI: The typical applicant is likely to be reasonably wealthy (ie able to afford a high single premium in order to protect their assets) and financially sophisticated.
For example, they are prepared to pay a high single premium, and may be seeking this insurance in order to protect their assets.
However they will be elderly and already in need of care.

Financial advisers could sell this business, but specialist brokers may be best. For example, they will need to have the skills to obtain the necessary medical underwriting information, some of which may be sensitive.
The target customers will also value face-to-face advice, and may wish to have someone who will 'shop around' to get the best price for them.
Brokers with whom customers are in regular contact may provide the best opportunities here.
The insurer could develop its own sales force, but this would be costly, and risky if it proves to be unpopular.
It may be difficult to find the people requiring this product, as the insurer does not have an existing sales force with which to cross sell.
Tied agents may be persuaded to sell this business if the terms were right.
A tie-up with an organisation providing long-term care, or providing advice to elderly people, would be ideal if it could be arranged.
Group IP insurance: Most group health business is sold through large financial advisers, such as employee benefit consultants. Many of these advisers may only deal with groups, and so they will be new to the insurer. It will therefore need to develop relationships with them.

A deal with a tied agent or direct marketing are unlikely to be successful, as most employers are likely to wish the flexibility of being able to get the best deal available in the market for their money.
Similarly, it is unlikely that the insurer will be able to justify the cost of a new sales force in this specialist market.
Unit-linked MME insurance: The complexity of the product rules out a simple direct marketing approach, and will make it difficult to use tied agents.
The viability of the insurer establishing its own sales force for this product depends greatly on future demand for the product - the company will need to wait and see how this develops.
Financial advisers would be the best channel. The product is likely to appeal to the financially-sophisticated individual.
The insurer already has brand awareness with financial advisers and most will be familiar with unit-linked products.
The product may appeal to brokers looking for a (slightly different) alternative to PMI.

(iv).question: The main costs and risks associated with developing and launching the product

Immediate needs LTCI: If the insurer is new to writing annuities there will be significant costs associated with setting up new systems, eg to pay annuities.
Even if the insurer is already writing annuity business, there will be changes required in the areas of underwriting, marketing, sales and pricing.
Careful underwriting and assessment of risk will be required to price the product accurately, and this may be essential if the product is to be competitive. This will incur costs.
If underwriting is not sufficiently accurate or detailed, the insurer may be exposed to anti-selection risk.

Data used for pricing (or making the decision whether or not to launch the product) may be inappropriate for use on this type of business (eg based on normal, rather than impaired mortality), and the adjustments made may be inaccurate.
Demand for this product may be significantly lower than expected, in which case development costs in launching product may not be recouped.
Requirement for capital will partly depend on the regulatory reserving and solvency capital requirements, but it will be significant.
Brokers will need to be persuaded to sell the new product, and trained to deal with the new underwriting requirements, which will increase costs.
This is a fairly specialised market, and significant costs may be required in identifying, contacting and attracting the potential customers.
There is a risk that the sales method used may not be able to reach those people actually requiring the new product.
Sales and underwriting procedures may be too long or complicated, deterring some from purchasing the product.
The pricing structure may not be easily adaptable to changes, eg in competitors' rates or investment conditions, in order to remain competitive.
There is a marketing risk in that customers think increasing payments will cover the actual cost of care, and there may be ill-will if they do not.
The level of benefits provided by the State will also affect demand. For example, there will be less need for the product if the State's LTC benefits are more generous.
The insurer is exposed to longevity risk in that people may live for longer than allowed for in the pricing assumptions.
There is a risk that inflation will be greater than expected. However, if inflation is high, this may be partially offset by better investment performance.
Investment risk should be reduced by careful matching to liabilities.

Data used for assessing the viability of the launch may be inappropriate for use on this type of business (eg based on normal, rather than impaired mortality) and the adjustments made may be inaccurate.
Group IP insurance: The insurer is likely to be selling individual IP business and so similar systems could be used for this product. However, there will still be many changes required in order to deal with group business.
For example claim definitions, product design, pricing, claims handling and underwriting may all be different from individual IP products.
The ongoing costs of underwriting will be lower than for other products, as only cases above the free cover limits are underwritten.
However, there will be development costs of setting the free cover limits as this will be new to the insurer. Advice may be sought (eg from reinsurers).

Large brokers will shop around to get the best deals, and so it will be important that the insurer markets its products actively, keeps prices competitive, and also maintain a high level of service to both the broker and the customer.
Also, a high level of commission may need to be offered early on, to encourage sales and build up the business.
These brokers will wish to deal closely with the clients, acting as a go-between for all communication with the insurer. It is possible that they may carry out much of the administration.
Whilst the last point may reduce ongoing expenses for the insurer, the other changes to the insurer's current procedures are likely to result in significant development costs overall.
Sales volumes may be lower than expected, particularly if the large brokers cannot be persuaded to buy into the product.
The market for group IP is dependent on external factors such as the strength of the economy or business optimism.
There is a risk that a future recession, for example, results in a reduced demand for group IP when the product is being launched.
There are political influences too. Changes in State provision of sickness and disability benefits may affect demand for group IP and the future experience.
If the tax advantages of providing IP are changed this could reduce demand for the product.
The insurer should make sure it has a wide spread of different risks, otherwise there is an accumulation risk.

For example, if the insurer has a concentration of a certain occupation, and there are subsequently a large number of claims as a result of the type of work carried out (eg lots of teachers suffering stress-related illnesses following changes to exam structures).
There is a significant persistency risk in that employers may change insurer every year or two.
There is also a risk that the insurer will lose direct contact with the customer, making it more difficult to control claims and cross-sell products.
Morbidity experience is uncertain, could be very different for group business, and may deteriorate in time.
However, premiums can be changed on each renewal to reflect this, and the groups can be experience rated.
Unit-linked MME insurance: Significant development costs will be required. Unit-linked systems will need to be adapted.
New sales and claims underwriting procedures will need to be developed. Product design, policy wording and pricing will all be new to the insurer. This will incur costs.
Capital requirements are likely to be low, although this depends on the charging structure.
This is an unusual and new product to the market. Demand will be very uncertain if there are no existing market figures to assess likely demand.
Therefore, there is a big risk that the product will not appeal to the market (either customers or sellers), resulting in poor sales.
Improvements to benefits or pricing are unlikely to increase demand if there is no competition.

Unit-linked products may be out of favour if equity markets have performed poorly in recent years, and so this will adversely affect demand unless this can be addressed, eg by promoting fixed-interest funds.

Claim incidence will rise sharply with age. The required level premium to cover this increasing risk over the term of the contract may be too high to appeal to customers; similarly increases in future premiums may be unpopular.
Expenses could also be high, which will push up charges, and hence premiums. Resulting volumes may not be sufficient to cover expenses at an attractive price.
Future claims experience will be uncertain on this new product. Although charges can be reviewed as a result, increases will be limited by consumer demand and customers' reasonable expectations.
In addition, there is a risk that back-end underwriting will not identify all claims for pre-existing conditions, and so there will be selection against the insurer, impacting on claims costs.
The investment risk will be borne by the customer, except to the extent of any fund management changes.
General points for all products: The cost of providing the product disclosure and sales training required by the sales regulations will need to be allowed for.
Systems will need to be developed to provide this for the new products.
There is always the risk that a new product may be too successful, using up capital from elsewhere in the business.

This is unlikely to have a significant effect because the products are quite specialist and not significantly capital intensive (compared to, for example, conventional life savings products), unless volumes are much higher than anticipated.
However, more sales than expected may lead to resourcing problems for the insurer, compromising service standards.
